lessons.title: Buying the Basics
lessons.scene: You walk to the kirana (provision) shop downstairs for some essentials. The shopkeeper, Murthy, is stacking biscuit packets behind the counter.

steps.speaker: Murthy
steps.english: Come sir, what do you need?

steps.prompt: Ask for one kilo of rice and a packet of milk

choices.kannada: Ondu kilo akki mattu ondu paaket haalu kodi.
choices.script: ಒಂದು ಕಿಲೋ ಅಕ್ಕಿ ಮತ್ತು ಒಂದು ಪಾಕೆಟ್ ಹಾಲು ಕೊಡಿ.
choices.pronunciation: ON-du KI-lo AK-ki mat-tu ON-du paa-ket HAA-lu KO-di
choices.english: Give one kilo of rice and one packet of milk.

choices.kannada: Ondu kilo haalu mattu paaket akki kodi.
choices.script: ಒಂದು ಕಿಲೋ ಹಾಲು ಮತ್ತು ಪಾಕೆಟ್ ಅಕ್ಕಿ ಕೊಡಿ.
choices.pronunciation: ON-du KI-lo HAA-lu mat-tu paa-ket AK-ki KO-di
choices.english: Give one kilo of milk and a packet of rice.
choices.hint: You swapped them! Rice (akki) comes by the kilo, milk (haalu) by the packet.
'Beda' refuses both items. To ask for something use 'kodi' (give) or just name it with the quantity.

wrongReaction.speaker: Murthy
wrongReaction.kannada: Akki eshtu beku saar, haalu eshtu?
wrongReaction.script: ಅಕ್ಕಿ ಎಷ್ಟು ಬೇಕು ಸಾರ್, ಹಾಲು ಎಷ್ಟು?
wrongReaction.pronunciation: AK-ki ESH-tu BEH-ku saar, HAA-lu ESH-tu
wrongReaction.english: How much rice do you need sir, how much milk?

steps.grammarTip: Order of words is flexible in Kannada, but the verb usually comes last: '[quantity] [item] kodi'. 'Kodi' (give) politely requests; you can also just name what you want and the shopkeeper understands.

steps.prompt: Add: you also want half a litre of cooking oil

choices.english: Give half a litre of oil too.
Give half a kilo of water.
choices.hint: 'Neeru' is water, not oil. Oil is 'enne', and liquids go by 'litre'.
That declines the oil. Use 'kooda' (also) to add it: 'enne kooda kodi'.

correctReaction.speaker: Murthy
correctReaction.kannada: Sari saar. Ottu nooru aippattu rupayi aytu.
correctReaction.script: ಸರಿ ಸಾರ್. ಒಟ್ಟು ನೂರ ಐವತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿ ಆಯ್ತು.
correctReaction.pronunciation: SA-ri saar. OT-tu NOO-ra AI-vat-tu ru-PAA-yi AAY-tu
correctReaction.english: Okay sir. The total comes to one hundred fifty rupees.

wrongReaction.speaker: Murthy
wrongReaction.english: Do you want oil or not, tell me sir.

steps.grammarTip: 'Kooda' = 'also/too' and goes right after the thing you're adding: 'enne kooda' = oil also. Note 'nooru aippattu' (150) — Kannada builds numbers as hundred + fifty, said together.

lessons.title: Is it Available? Getting Change
lessons.scene: Next evening you return for eggs and bread. It's busy and Murthy is dealing with another customer. You wait, then ask.

choices.hint: That asks where eggs are 'going'. To ask if something is in stock, use 'ide-ya?' (is it there?).
That's asking him if HE wants eggs. To check availability: 'motte ide-ya?'.

correctReaction.speaker: Murthy
correctReaction.english: Yes, available sir. How many do you need?

wrongReaction.speaker: Murthy
wrongReaction.english: Do you want eggs sir? We have them.

steps.grammarTip: Add '-aa/-ya' to the end of a statement to turn it into a yes/no question: 'ide' (it is there) → 'ide-ya?' (is it there?). The answer is 'ide' (yes, there is) or 'illa' (no, there isn't).

steps.speaker: You
steps.kannada: Aaru motte mattu ondu bread kodi.
steps.script: ಆರು ಮೊಟ್ಟೆ ಮತ್ತು ಒಂದು ಬ್ರೆಡ್ ಕೊಡಿ.
steps.pronunciation: AA-ru MOT-te mat-tu ON-du bread KO-di
steps.english: Give six eggs and one bread.

steps.speaker: Murthy
steps.kannada: Embattu rupayi saar. Chillare illa, irutta?
steps.script: ಎಂಬತ್ತು ರೂಪಾಯಿ ಸಾರ್. ಚಿಲ್ಲರೆ ಇಲ್ಲ, ಇರುತ್ತಾ?
steps.pronunciation: EM-bat-tu ru-PAA-yi saar. CHIL-la-re IL-la, i-rut-ta
steps.english: Eighty rupees sir. I have no change, do you have it?

steps.prompt: Tell him you have exact change and will give it

choices.english: I have change, I'll give it right here.
I have no change, leave it.
choices.hint: He asked if YOU have change and you said no. To say you do have it: 'chillare ide'.

choices.english: Shall I give YOU some change?
choices.hint: Close, but mixes it up. Simply confirm 'chillare ide' (I have change) and pay.

correctReaction.speaker: Murthy
correctReaction.english: Very good sir. Thank you, do come again!

wrongReaction.speaker: Murthy
wrongReaction.english: Then give a hundred, I'll find change.

vocab.english: I'll just give it (and be done)

steps.grammarTip: Kannadigas often add 'bidu' (to leave/let) after a verb to mean 'just do it / finish it off': 'kotti bidtini' = I'll just hand it over. It makes the action sound easy and complete.
